items.prompt: What score do you need to pass the CCA-F exam?
choices: 720 out of 1000
60 out of 100
80%, no curve
items.explanation: The CCA-F is 60 scenario-based questions in 120 minutes; you need 720/1000. Scoring is weighted, so the heavy domains (Agentic Architecture at 27%) matter most.

items.prompt: Which domain carries the MOST weight on the exam?
choices: Agentic Architecture & Orchestration (27%)
Prompt Engineering (20%)
Tool Design & MCP (18%)
Context Management (15%)
items.explanation: Agentic Architecture & Orchestration is the backbone of the exam at 27% — more than any other single domain. Invest study time here first.

items.prompt: An API response comes back with stop_reason = "tool_use". What does Claude want you to do?
choices: Execute the requested tool(s) and return the result(s) to continue the loop
Stop — the turn is complete
Increase max_tokens and retry
Summarize the conversation
items.explanation: stop_reason "tool_use" means Claude is pausing to call a tool. You run the tool, append a tool_result, and call the API again. The loop ends only on stop_reason "end_turn".

items.prompt: Which stop_reason value signals the agentic loop is DONE (no more tool calls needed)?
items.explanation: "end_turn" = Claude finished its turn with no tool call pending. "tool_use" = it wants a tool. Other values: max_tokens, stop_sequence.

items.prompt: In a hub-and-spoke coordinator–subagent architecture, what is the coordinator responsible for?
choices: Managing subagent communication, task decomposition, error handling, and result aggregation
Executing every tool call itself so subagents stay simple
Sharing its full conversation history with every subagent
Nothing — subagents talk directly to each other
items.explanation: The central coordinator owns delegation, comms, error handling, and aggregation. Subagents do scoped work and report back — they don't talk peer-to-peer.

items.prompt: Three of these are TRUE about subagents. Pick the statement that is WRONG.
choices: Subagents inherit the coordinator's full conversation history
Subagents operate with isolated context scoped to their task
Subagents return results to the coordinator for aggregation
Isolating subagent context protects the token budget
items.explanation: WRONG: subagents do NOT inherit the coordinator's full history. They run with isolated, task-scoped context — that keeps each one focused and protects the token budget.

items.prompt: When is splitting work into MULTIPLE agents justified rather than one agent?
choices: When distinct security/tool boundaries or context isolation justify it
Always — more agents always perform better
Only when the prompt exceeds 200K tokens
Never — a single agent is always simpler and better
items.explanation: Multi-agent adds coordination cost. Justify it with real boundaries: different tool/permission scopes, isolated context, or parallel decomposition — not 'more is better'.

items.prompt: When should you use ADAPTIVE dynamic decomposition instead of a fixed sequential pipeline?
choices: When subtasks are discovered as the agent investigates and can't be predetermined
When you want the cheapest possible run
When the task has exactly three known steps
When you need deterministic, repeatable output every time
items.explanation: Fixed pipelines fit known, ordered steps. Adaptive decomposition fits open-ended investigation where new subtasks emerge from what the agent finds.

items.prompt: Explain WHY subagents should run with isolated context instead of inheriting the coordinator's entire history.
items.answer: Isolated context keeps each subagent focused on its specific task, avoids polluting it with irrelevant history, conserves the token budget (full history would bloat every call and cost more), reduces the chance of confusion or contradictory instructions, and lets subagents run in parallel cleanly. The coordinator passes only the scoped inputs each subagent needs.
rubric: focus / relevance to the scoped task
items.explanation: Key points: focus, token/cost savings, no context pollution, parallelism, pass-only-what's-needed.

items.prompt: You must GUARANTEE a validation step runs before a deploy step in an agentic workflow. Best mechanism?
choices: A programmatic hook / prerequisite gate that enforces it in code
A polite instruction in the system prompt asking Claude to remember
Raising the temperature so Claude explores more
Adding more few-shot examples of the right order
items.explanation: For hard guarantees, enforce with deterministic hooks/gates — not model judgment. The exam rewards 'rules over model discretion' for critical multi-step guarantees.

items.prompt: For a CRITICAL step that must always happen in an exact order, you should prefer programmatic ___ over relying on the model's judgment.
items.explanation: Deterministic rules/gates/hooks give guarantees; model judgment is probabilistic. Use rules for must-happen steps, model reasoning for open-ended ones.

items.prompt: When should you call messages.stream() instead of messages.create()?
choices: For real-time UX or long responses where you show output as it generates
When you need a guaranteed single JSON blob for parsing
Only inside CI/CD pipelines
When the response must be cached
items.explanation: stream() = token-by-token, best for responsive UIs and long outputs. create() = one complete response, best for short/deterministic calls you parse downstream.

items.prompt: Your agent gets HTTP 429 from the Claude API. What happened?
choices: Rate limit — you're sending too many requests; back off and retry
The server is overloaded; nothing you can do
Your API key is invalid
The context window was exceeded
items.explanation: 429 = rate limited (slow down, exponential backoff). 529 = overloaded (Anthropic-side; also back off). Don't hammer in a tight retry loop.

items.prompt: Which HTTP status code does the Claude API return when it is OVERLOADED?
items.explanation: 529 = overloaded (Anthropic-side). 429 = rate limit (your request volume). Both call for backoff + retry.

items.prompt: An orchestrator delegates to 4 subagents and one returns a transient error. Best handling?
choices: Retry that subagent with backoff, aggregate the partial results, and escalate only if it persists
Immediately fail the entire run
Silently drop the failed subagent's work and report success
Restart all 4 subagents from scratch
items.explanation: Graceful degradation: isolate the failure, retry with backoff, keep good partial results, surface uncertainty, and escalate only if recovery fails.

items.prompt: Pick the statement about the agentic loop that is WRONG.
choices: The loop ends as soon as the model returns any text content
Each tool_use must be followed by a tool_result before the next call
The loop continues while stop_reason is tool_use
The loop ends on stop_reason end_turn
items.explanation: WRONG: text alone doesn't end the loop. The loop continues while Claude requests tools (tool_use) and ends on end_turn.

items.prompt: Scenario: a coordinator delegates to search, analysis, synthesis, and report subagents. The analysis subagent returns a partial failure. Describe how to handle it gracefully.
items.answer: Don't fail the whole run. The coordinator should catch the failure, retry the analysis subagent with exponential backoff, and if it still fails, proceed with the partial results it does have while clearly flagging the gap/uncertainty in the final report. Aggregate what the other subagents produced, degrade gracefully, and escalate to a human only if the missing piece is critical and unrecoverable.
rubric: do not fail the entire run
escalate to human only if critical/unrecoverable
items.explanation: Key points: don't fail everything, retry w/ backoff, use partials, flag uncertainty, escalate if critical.

items.prompt: What is the Model Context Protocol (MCP)?
choices: An open standard for secure, two-way connections between Claude and external tools/data
A prompt-formatting language for system prompts
Anthropic's proprietary token-compression format
A replacement for the Messages API
items.explanation: MCP is an open standard that lets you build secure two-way connections between a model and external tools, data sources, and services.

items.prompt: Claude keeps calling the wrong tool. Two tools have similar, vague descriptions. Best fix?
choices: Sharpen and differentiate the descriptions so each clearly says when to use it
Raise the temperature so Claude explores more options
Merge them into one giant tool
Add the word 'IMPORTANT' to both descriptions
items.explanation: Misrouting is usually a description problem. Precise, differentiated descriptions stating WHEN to use each tool fix it far better than temperature or emphasis hacks.

items.prompt: Pick the WRONG statement about tool design.
choices: Always consolidate every capability into one monolithic tool
Split tools by purpose when their boundaries and inputs differ
Scope each agent to only the tools its role needs
Clear tool descriptions reduce misrouting
items.explanation: WRONG: monolithic tools blur descriptions and weaken least-privilege scoping. Split purpose-specific tools; consolidate only when they're genuinely the same action.

items.prompt: Applying least-privilege to an agent's tools means:
choices: Give the agent only the tools its role actually needs
Give every agent all tools for flexibility
Give tools only to the coordinator
Disable tools and rely on prompts
items.explanation: Scoped/least-privilege access limits blast radius and misrouting: each agent gets only role-relevant tools.

items.prompt: Which boolean field in a structured tool error tells Claude whether retrying the call could succeed?
items.explanation: isRetryable signals whether a retry makes sense (true for transient errors, false for validation/permission errors). Pair it with isError and an error category.

items.prompt: A well-designed structured tool error categorizes failures. Which set of categories matches best practice?
choices: transient, validation, business, permission
red, yellow, green
fatal, nonfatal
client, server
items.explanation: Categorizing as transient / validation / business / permission lets the agent decide: retry (transient), fix inputs (validation), stop (business), or escalate (permission).

items.prompt: Which flag does a tool result set to indicate the call FAILED rather than succeeded?
items.explanation: isError = true marks the tool result as an error so Claude handles it as a failure (vs treating the payload as a normal result).

items.prompt: You have ONE tool `manage_user` that can create, update, delete, AND search users. Explain why splitting it into separate tools could help the agent.
items.answer: Separate tools get sharper, unambiguous descriptions, so Claude is less likely to misroute (e.g. call delete when it meant update). It enables least-privilege scoping — a read-only agent gets only `search_user` and physically cannot delete. Each tool can have purpose-specific inputs and error semantics. It also makes auditing and permissioning cleaner. Consolidate only when actions are genuinely the same.
rubric: least-privilege / scoped access (e.g. read-only can't delete)
items.explanation: Key points: clearer descriptions, least-privilege scoping, purpose-specific I/O, safer permissions.

items.prompt: After a tool runs, what must happen for the agentic loop to continue correctly?
choices: Its result is appended to the conversation as a tool_result tied to the originating tool_use id
The result is discarded and Claude re-derives it
The whole conversation is summarized before continuing
The result is stored only in a separate log file
items.explanation: Tool results must be fed back into the conversation as a tool_result matching the tool_use id, so Claude can reason over what the tool returned.

items.prompt: You're composing several MCP servers into one agent. A key risk to manage is:
choices: Overlapping/ambiguous tool descriptions causing misrouting, and over-broad access
Running out of disk space
MCP only allows one server per agent
MCP tools cannot return structured errors
items.explanation: With many servers, keep descriptions distinct and scope access per role. Overlap causes misrouting; over-broad access violates least privilege.

items.prompt: What is the purpose of a CLAUDE.md file?
choices: Persistent project instructions/conventions Claude reads across sessions
A changelog of Claude's edits
A list of installed MCP servers only
A one-time prompt used and then discarded
items.explanation: CLAUDE.md encodes durable project rules (architecture, standards, conventions) that persist across sessions, so you don't re-explain them each time.

items.prompt: Pick the WRONG statement about CLAUDE.md.
choices: It applies only to the current session and is forgotten afterward
It can exist at user, project, and directory levels
More specific (closer) files refine broader ones
It's where you encode standards Claude should always follow
items.explanation: WRONG: CLAUDE.md PERSISTS across sessions — that's the whole point. It also nests: user-level, project-level, and directory-level, with closer files refining broader ones.

items.prompt: Which Claude Code flag runs it non-interactively (headless) for CI/CD pipelines?
items.explanation: The -p (--print) flag switches Claude Code from interactive to headless/non-interactive mode. Forgetting it in CI is a classic tested anti-pattern.

items.prompt: Pick the WRONG statement about running Claude Code in CI/CD.
choices: Interactive mode is required inside CI pipelines
Use the -p/--print flag for non-interactive runs
--output-format json gives machine-parseable output
You can automate reviews, tests, and PR feedback
items.explanation: WRONG: CI needs HEADLESS mode (-p/--print), not interactive. Pair it with --output-format json so the pipeline can parse results.

items.prompt: Why pass --output-format json to Claude Code in a pipeline?
choices: So downstream pipeline steps can reliably parse the output
To make the output prettier for humans
To enable interactive mode
To reduce token cost
items.explanation: JSON output is machine-parseable, so CI steps (gates, comment bots, dashboards) can consume Claude Code's results deterministically.

items.prompt: When is PLAN mode the better choice over direct execution?
choices: For complex/ambiguous multi-step changes you want to review before they run
For a trivial one-line edit
Whenever you're in a hurry
Only in CI pipelines
items.explanation: Plan mode surfaces the approach for review before acting — ideal for risky/complex/ambiguous work. Direct execution fits small, well-defined changes.

items.prompt: Which set best describes Claude Code hook types?
choices: pre-tool, post-tool, and on-error hooks
morning, noon, night hooks
GET, POST, DELETE hooks
compile, test, deploy hooks
items.explanation: Hooks fire around tool execution (pre-tool, post-tool) and on failures (on-error), letting you enforce gates, validation, and logging deterministically.

items.prompt: What do git worktrees enable for Claude Code agent work?
choices: Parallel agent tasks in isolated working dirs/branches without collisions
Faster token streaming
Automatic prompt caching
A way to skip the -p flag
items.explanation: Worktrees give each parallel agent its own isolated checkout, so simultaneous tasks don't clobber each other's files.

items.prompt: Which Claude Code slash command lets you inspect current context-window usage?
items.explanation: /context shows context usage. Other common ones: /plan (plan mode) and /tools (available tools).

items.prompt: Your team wants Claude Code to ALWAYS run tests before committing and ALWAYS use TypeScript for new files. How do you encode this so it persists?
items.answer: Put the durable conventions in a project-level CLAUDE.md ('use TypeScript for new files', 'run tests before committing') so they persist across every session. For the must-happen guarantee (tests before commit), back it with a deterministic hook/prerequisite gate (e.g. a pre-commit or pre-tool hook) rather than trusting the model to remember every time. Don't rely on per-prompt reminders.
rubric: CLAUDE.md project-level rules for the conventions
hook / prerequisite gate to ENFORCE tests-before-commit
don't rely on per-prompt reminders / model memory
items.explanation: Key points: CLAUDE.md for persistence, hook/gate to enforce the hard guarantee, not per-prompt memory.

items.prompt: An Agent Skill in Claude Code is best described as:
choices: A packaged, reusable capability (instructions + optional tools) Claude can invoke
A paid add-on subscription
A type of MCP error category
A synonym for the context window
items.explanation: Skills bundle reusable instructions/capability so Claude can apply a known workflow consistently, versioned and shareable across projects.

items.prompt: You need reliable, machine-readable JSON output from Claude. Best approach?
choices: Define a JSON schema and use tool_use/structured output, then validate
Ask for JSON in plain prose and hope it parses
Set temperature to 1.0 for creativity
Request Markdown and convert it later
items.explanation: Schema-enforced structured output (via tool_use) plus validation is the reliable path. Prose 'please return JSON' is fragile and breaks downstream parsing.

items.prompt: Few-shot examples help MOST when:
choices: The task is ambiguous or you need to pin an exact format and edge-case handling
The task is a trivial single-word lookup
You want to reduce the prompt to zero examples
You need maximum randomness
items.explanation: Few-shot shines for ambiguous tasks and locking output format/edge cases. For trivial tasks they add cost without benefit.

items.prompt: What's the name of the pattern where you validate Claude's structured output and re-prompt it when validation fails?
items.explanation: A validation-retry loop checks the output against the schema/rules and re-prompts on failure — essential for production-grade structured extraction.

items.prompt: XML tags in a prompt are especially useful for:
choices: Clearly delimiting sections of the input/output (e.g. <document>, <answer>)
Guaranteeing valid machine-parseable output for downstream systems
Reducing token cost to near zero
Replacing the need for a system prompt
items.explanation: XML tags structure text and mark boundaries Claude respects well. For machine-consumable data you still want JSON-schema/tool_use output.

items.prompt: You must extract structured data from 50,000 documents, cost-efficiently and asynchronously. Which API fits best?
choices: The Message Batches (Batch) API
messages.stream() in a tight loop
The MCP protocol
A single 50,000-document prompt
items.explanation: The Batch API handles high-volume async jobs at lower cost — ideal for large-scale structured extraction where latency isn't critical.

items.prompt: Pick the WRONG statement about prompt engineering.
choices: Vague instructions outperform explicit criteria
Explicit success criteria beat vague instructions
Role/task/context framing improves reliability
Guardrails and constraints reduce off-target output
items.explanation: WRONG: explicit, criteria-rich instructions consistently beat vague ones. Tell Claude exactly what 'good' looks like.

items.prompt: What does the temperature parameter control?
choices: How random vs deterministic the sampling is (lower = more deterministic)
The maximum output length
Which model version is used
The size of the context window
items.explanation: Temperature tunes randomness. Lower (near 0) = focused/deterministic; higher = more varied/creative. top_p and top_k also shape sampling.

items.prompt: For the most deterministic, repeatable extraction output, roughly what temperature do you set?
items.explanation: Temperature 0 (or near-0) gives the most deterministic output — what you want for structured extraction you parse downstream.

items.prompt: Scenario: extract {name, date, amount} from messy contracts as valid JSON with minimal hallucination. Outline your prompt strategy.
items.answer: Use a JSON schema via tool_use/structured output so the shape is enforced. Give explicit criteria for each field and instruct 'return null if a field is absent' to curb hallucination. Add one or two few-shot examples covering tricky/edge cases. Set temperature to 0 for determinism. Wrap it in a validation-retry loop: validate against the schema and re-prompt on failure. For volume, run it through the Batch API.
rubric: explicit per-field criteria + 'null if absent' to reduce hallucination
items.explanation: Key points: schema/tool_use, explicit criteria + null-if-absent, few-shot, temp 0, validation-retry.

items.prompt: Why do idempotent, deterministic output formats matter for production pipelines?
choices: Downstream systems can parse and process them reliably and repeatably
They make responses longer
They increase model creativity
They eliminate the need for any validation
items.explanation: Deterministic, stable formats let downstream code parse safely every time — critical when Claude's output feeds another system.

items.prompt: Asking Claude to reason step-by-step (chain-of-thought) helps MOST with:
choices: Multi-step reasoning or math/logic problems
A simple keyword lookup
Reducing token cost
Forcing JSON output
items.explanation: CoT improves multi-step reasoning by giving the model room to work. It adds little to trivial lookups and increases tokens, so use it where reasoning is needed.

items.prompt: What is the standard Claude context window size referenced in the CCA-F guides?
choices: ~200K tokens
~4K tokens
~8K tokens
Unlimited
items.explanation: The exam references the ~200K-token window. Architecting around it (summarize, truncate, selectively inject) is a core reliability skill.

items.prompt: A long-running conversation is approaching the token limit. Best strategy?
choices: Summarize older turns and selectively inject only what's still relevant
Send the full history every call until it errors
Delete the system prompt to save room
Switch to a smaller model mid-conversation
items.explanation: Summarization, truncation, and selective injection keep critical info while staying within budget. Blindly resending everything wastes tokens and eventually fails.

items.prompt: What feature caches a repeated prompt prefix to cut cost and latency on repeated calls?
items.explanation: Prompt caching reuses a cached prefix (via cache-control), reducing cost and latency when the same large context is sent repeatedly.

items.prompt: Prompt caching primarily reduces:
choices: Cost and latency on repeated prompt prefixes
Hallucination rate
The number of tools an agent can use
The passing score needed on the exam
items.explanation: Caching a stable prefix avoids reprocessing it each call — cheaper and faster. It doesn't change accuracy or tool limits.

items.prompt: Best way to handle transient API errors (429/529) in production?
choices: Retry with exponential backoff (and jitter)
Retry instantly in a tight loop until it works
Give up and return an error to the user immediately
Switch API keys on every error
items.explanation: Exponential backoff (ideally with jitter) spaces retries so you recover without hammering an already-stressed endpoint.

items.prompt: Pick the WRONG statement about reliability.
choices: On a 429, immediately retry in a tight loop with no delay
Use exponential backoff for transient errors
Evals catch output-quality regressions over time
Escalate to a human when the agent can't make progress
items.explanation: WRONG: a tight no-delay retry loop on 429 makes rate limiting worse. Back off exponentially.

items.prompt: Which is a valid trigger for an agent to escalate to a human?
choices: The user asks for a human, a policy gap appears, or the agent can't make meaningful progress
The conversation exceeds two turns
Any time a tool is called
Whenever temperature is above 0
items.explanation: Escalate on explicit user request, policy/coverage gaps, or genuine inability to progress — not on arbitrary turn counts.

items.prompt: Scenario: in a multi-agent system, context is being lost when one agent hands off to another, causing errors. How do you preserve context integrity across handoffs?
items.answer: Pass an explicit, structured handoff summary that carries the critical state (decisions, key facts, current goal, constraints) rather than dumping or truncating the full raw history. Define a clear contract for what each handoff must include, validate that required fields are present at the boundary, and avoid lossy truncation that drops key facts. Keep the handoff payload focused so the receiving agent has exactly what it needs without context bloat.
rubric: explicit/structured handoff summary of critical state
pass key facts/decisions, not full raw history
validate required context at the boundary
avoid lossy truncation of critical info
items.explanation: Key points: structured handoff summary, carry critical state not raw dump, validate at boundary, no lossy truncation.

items.prompt: What do you call the practice of programmatically scoring Claude's outputs to catch quality regressions over time?
items.explanation: Evals (programmatic evaluations + regression checks) let you measure output quality objectively and catch regressions as prompts/models change.
